walkthrough.title: Select a Battle Scenario
walkthrough.text: Let's get Memoir '44 set up. First, open the scenario booklet and pick a battle. If this is your first game, start with the very first scenario: Pegasus Bridge on page 19. It was the opening engagement of D-Day and makes a perfect introductory battle. The scenario page shows you everything you need: the battlefield map, terrain placement, unit positions, how many Command cards each side gets, and who goes first.

walkthrough.title: Place the Battlefield Board
walkthrough.text: Take the double-sided battlefield board and place it in the center of the table. Check your scenario to see which side you need: countryside or beach landing. The board is 13 hexes wide by 9 hexes deep. You will notice two red dotted lines dividing it into three sections: a left flank, a center, and a right flank. Each player sees the flanks from their own perspective, so your left flank is your opponent's right flank. For Pegasus Bridge, use the countryside face.

walkthrough.title: Place the Terrain Hexes
walkthrough.text: Now lay down the terrain hex pieces as shown on the scenario map. The game includes 44 double-sided terrain hex pieces in total. Match each hex type and position to the battle map exactly. For Pegasus Bridge, you will place 20 river hexes first, then 4 village hexes, and finally 9 forest hexes. Take your time getting these right -- the terrain drives the entire battle.

walkthrough.title: Add Obstacles and Bridges
walkthrough.text: Next, place any fixed obstacles like bunkers and bridges, plus removable obstacles like barbed wire and sandbags, as indicated by your scenario. The game includes 4 double-sided rectangular bunker and bridge tiles and 18 man-made obstacle pieces per army. For Pegasus Bridge, place two bridges (one over each river) and set up four barbed wire obstacles and one sandbag placement to protect the bridge perimeter.

walkthrough.title: Deploy Your Units
walkthrough.text: Now place the miniature figures on the board, matching each unit's position to the scenario map. Each army bag contains 42 Infantry figures, 24 Armored vehicles, and 6 Artillery pieces. A standard Infantry unit has 4 figures, an Armor unit has 3 figures, and an Artillery unit has 2 figures. A quick trick: drop one figure per hex first to mark positions, then fill each unit to its proper strength. Green figures are Allied Forces and blue-gray figures are Axis/German Forces.

walkthrough.title: Add Special Unit Badges and Victory Medals
walkthrough.text: If the scenario calls for Special Forces or Elite units, place the corresponding Special Forces badges on those hexes to distinguish them from regular troops. The game includes 14 Special Forces badges in total. Also place any Victory Medals on specific battlefield objectives as required by the scenario's special rules. For Pegasus Bridge, place an Allied Victory Medal on each of the two bridges. The game includes 10 double-sided Victory Medals.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Summary Cards
walkthrough.text: Place the terrain summary cards that correspond to the terrain types in your scenario alongside the board for easy reference. For Pegasus Bridge, you need the Woods, Towns and Villages, and Rivers summary cards. Also lay out the Unit summary card (double-sided with Special Units on the back) and the Obstacle summary card (also double-sided). The game includes 7 terrain summary cards, 1 unit summary card, and 1 obstacle summary card -- 9 summary cards total.

walkthrough.title: Assemble the Cardholders
walkthrough.text: Each army bag includes 3 cardholder sections that snap together to form a card stand. Assemble them and place the completed cardholders on the board map's edges. These are not strictly necessary for a 2-player game, but they are particularly useful when playing in teams of multiple players per side, or in a teaching environment where players want to point at and discuss card options together.

walkthrough.title: Choose Sides and Seat Players
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a side -- Allies or Axis -- and sits in front of the board accordingly. Since most scenarios give a historical advantage to one side, the rulebook strongly recommends match play: play the scenario once, then swap sides and play it again. The winner of the match is the player who earns the most total Victory Medals across both games. This balances any inherent scenario advantage.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal Command Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the Command card deck thoroughly. The deck contains 60 Command cards: 40 Section cards and 20 Tactic cards. Deal Command cards to each player according to the scenario's briefing notes. Place them in your cardholder, keeping them secret from your opponent. For Pegasus Bridge, the Allied Commander-in-Chief receives 6 Command cards while the German General starts with only 2. Place the remainder of the deck face down alongside the board within easy reach of both players.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Battle Dice
walkthrough.text: Each side takes 4 of the 8 Battle dice included in the game. The dice have six faces: Infantry (appears twice), Armor, Grenade, Star, and Flag. Keep your dice handy -- you will be rolling them every time you attack. You are now ready to begin.

walkthrough.title: Determine the Starting Player
walkthrough.text: Check the scenario's briefing notes to see which side goes first. For Pegasus Bridge, the British Major John Howard's forces begin play. The starting player takes the first turn, and then players alternate turns until one side wins. Let's go to war.

walkthrough.title: Object of the Game
walkthrough.text: The goal of Memoir '44 is simple: be the first player to collect a set number of Victory Medals, usually 4 to 6 depending on the scenario. You earn one Victory Medal each time you completely eliminate an enemy unit from the battlefield. When you wipe out the last figure in a unit, place that figure on one of the Medal Stands on your side of the board. Some scenarios also award bonus medals for capturing and holding specific terrain hexes or objectives on the map.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Players alternate turns. On your turn, you follow five steps in order. Step 1: Play a Command card from your hand. Step 2: Order your units based on that card. Step 3: Move all ordered units, one at a time. Step 4: Battle with each ordered unit, one at a time. Step 5: Draw a new Command card from the deck. Let's walk through each step in detail.

walkthrough.title: Step 1: Play a Command Card
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, choose one Command card from your hand and play it face up. Read it aloud so your opponent knows what you are doing. There are two types of Command cards. Section cards tell you which section of the battlefield (left flank, center, or right flank) you can issue orders in and how many units you can order. Tactic cards let you make special moves, battle in specific ways, or take special actions as described on the card. Some Tactic cards let you order units across all sections.

walkthrough.title: Step 2: Order Units
walkthrough.text: After playing your Command card, announce which units you are ordering. Only ordered units may move, battle, or take special actions this turn. A unit on a hex where the red dotted section line passes through may be ordered from either adjacent section. You may not give more than one order to each unit. If your card allows more orders in a section than you have units there, those extra orders are simply lost.

walkthrough.title: Step 3: Move Units
walkthrough.text: Move your ordered units one at a time, in any sequence you choose. You must complete one unit's movement before starting the next, and all movement must be finished before any battles begin. A unit may only move once per turn and does not have to move at all. Two units may never occupy the same hex, and you cannot move through hexes occupied by any unit, friendly or enemy. Figures in a unit must always stay together -- no splitting off or recombining with other units.

walkthrough.title: Infantry Movement
walkthrough.text: An ordered Infantry unit may move up to 1 hex and then battle, or move up to 2 hexes and not battle. This is the core trade-off for Infantry: mobility or firepower, but not both in the same turn. Special Forces Infantry (like US Rangers and British Commandos) break this rule -- they may move up to 2 hexes and still battle, though terrain restrictions still apply.

walkthrough.title: Armor Movement
walkthrough.text: An ordered Armor unit may move up to 3 hexes and still battle. Tanks are the fastest units on the battlefield and they do not have to sacrifice combat for speed. This makes Armor excellent for flanking maneuvers and rapid strikes.

walkthrough.title: Artillery Movement
walkthrough.text: An ordered Artillery unit may either move 1 hex or battle, but never both on the same turn. Artillery is your long-range firepower, but it is slow and immobile in combat. Position your artillery carefully at the start of the game because repositioning it costs you an entire turn of shooting.

walkthrough.title: Step 4: Battle
walkthrough.text: After all movement is complete, resolve battles one ordered unit at a time, in any order you choose. Each unit may only battle once per turn and does not have to battle at all. A unit may not split its Battle dice between multiple targets. Crucially, the number of casualties a unit has taken does not reduce its firepower -- a unit with a single figure left rolls the same number of dice as a unit at full strength.

walkthrough.title: Close Assault vs. Fire
walkthrough.text: There are two types of attack. A unit attacking an adjacent enemy hex is in Close Assault. A unit attacking an enemy more than 1 hex away is said to Fire at the target. This distinction matters because if your unit is adjacent to an enemy, it must Close Assault that enemy -- it cannot fire at a more distant target instead. Close Assault also triggers special rules like Taking Ground and Armor Overrun.

walkthrough.title: Infantry Battle Range
walkthrough.text: Infantry can target enemies up to 3 hexes away. The number of Battle dice rolled decreases with distance: 3 dice at 1 hex (Close Assault), 2 dice at 2 hexes, and 1 die at 3 hexes. Use the count-down method: place your finger on the first hex adjacent to your unit and count toward the target -- 3, 2, 1. The number you land on is the number of dice you roll.

walkthrough.title: Armor Battle Range
walkthrough.text: Armor can target enemies up to 3 hexes away, just like Infantry, but Armor always rolls 3 Battle dice regardless of distance. Whether it is a Close Assault or firing at maximum range, tanks hit just as hard. This consistent firepower combined with their speed makes Armor your most flexible combat unit.

walkthrough.title: Artillery Battle Range
walkthrough.text: Artillery has the longest range in the game at 6 hexes, but it cannot move and battle on the same turn. The dice count-down is 3, 3, 2, 2, 1, 1 -- meaning 3 dice at 1-2 hexes, 2 dice at 3-4 hexes, and 1 die at 5-6 hexes. Unlike Infantry and Armor, Artillery does not need line of sight to fire. It can shoot over forests, hills, and other obstacles.

walkthrough.title: Line of Sight
walkthrough.text: Before an Infantry or Armor unit can attack, it must have line of sight to the target. Imagine a line drawn from the center of the attacking hex to the center of the target hex. If any hex along that line contains an obstruction -- another unit (friend or foe), a forest, a town, a hedgerow, a hill, or a bunker -- the line of sight is blocked. The terrain in the target hex itself does not block line of sight. When the line runs along the edge of hexes containing obstructions, line of sight is only blocked if obstructions appear on both sides of the line. Artillery ignores line of sight entirely.

walkthrough.title: Terrain Battle Dice Reductions
walkthrough.text: Terrain protects defending units by reducing the attacker's dice. Each terrain type lists its reductions on the corresponding summary card. The general pattern: when attacking an enemy in a forest, hedgerow, town, or bunker, Infantry reduces its dice by 1 and Armor reduces by 2. Artillery dice are never reduced by terrain. When attacking uphill, Infantry and Armor each reduce by 1. Always check the summary cards for the specific terrain in play.

walkthrough.title: Resolving Battle Dice
walkthrough.text: Roll your Battle dice and read the results. Each die has six faces: Infantry (appears twice), Armor, Grenade, Star, and Flag. You score a hit for each symbol that matches the targeted unit type. An Infantry symbol hits Infantry, an Armor symbol hits Armor. A Grenade is a hit against any unit type -- Infantry, Armor, or Artillery. For each hit, remove one figure from the target unit. The Star symbol is a miss unless a specific Tactic card says otherwise. A Flag forces the target to retreat.

walkthrough.title: Retreat
walkthrough.text: After resolving all hits, resolve retreats. For each Flag rolled against a unit, that unit must retreat 1 hex toward its own side of the board. Two flags mean 2 hexes back, and so on. The retreating player chooses the retreat path, but the unit must always move toward its own board edge. A retreating unit may pass through terrain without stopping, but cannot retreat through impassable terrain, onto or through hexes occupied by other units, or off the board. If a unit cannot complete a retreat move, it loses one figure for each hex it cannot retreat. Some obstacles, like bunkers, sandbags, and hedgehogs, allow a unit to ignore the first flag rolled against it.

walkthrough.title: Taking Ground
walkthrough.text: When an ordered Infantry unit in Close Assault eliminates the enemy or forces it to retreat, the Infantry unit may advance into the vacated hex. This is called Taking Ground. Terrain movement and battle restrictions still apply when taking ground. Artillery may never take ground.

walkthrough.title: Armor Overrun
walkthrough.text: When an ordered Armor unit in Close Assault eliminates the enemy or forces it to retreat, it may advance into the vacated hex and then battle again. This is the powerful Armor Overrun. If the new position is adjacent to another enemy, this second battle must be a Close Assault. Otherwise, the Armor may fire at a distant target. A unit may Take Ground again after a successful Overrun, but each Armor unit may only perform one Overrun per turn. All battles, including Close Assault and Overrun, must be completed before the next unit may battle.

walkthrough.title: Step 5: Draw a Command Card
walkthrough.text: After resolving all battles, retreats, and overruns, discard the Command card you played this turn and draw a new one from the deck. Your turn is now over. If the draw deck runs out, shuffle the discard pile to form a new draw deck. Your opponent now takes their turn following the same five-step sequence. Play continues alternating turns until one player reaches the required number of Victory Medals.

summary.title: Object of the Game
bullets: First player to earn the required Victory Medals wins (usually 4-6, per scenario)
Earn 1 medal per enemy unit fully eliminated
Some scenarios award bonus medals for holding specific terrain objectives

Step 2: Order units
Step 3: Move ordered units (one at a time)
Step 4: Battle with ordered units (one at a time)
Step 5: Draw a new Command card

summary.title: Step 1: Play a Command Card
bullets: Play 1 card face up from your hand, read it aloud
Section cards: specify which section(s) and how many units to order
Tactic cards: special moves, battles, or actions as described on the card

summary.title: Step 2: Order Units
bullets: Announce which units receive orders based on your Command card
Only ordered units may move, battle, or take special actions
Max 1 order per unit; excess orders in a section are lost
Units on a section dividing line may be ordered from either section

summary.title: Step 3: Move Units
bullets: Move ordered units one at a time, finish all movement before battling
No stacking: 2 units cannot share a hex
Cannot move through occupied hexes (friend or foe)
Units may never split or recombine

summary.title: Infantry Movement
bullets: Move 1 hex and battle, OR move 2 hexes and not battle
Special Forces Infantry: move 2 hexes and still battle
Terrain restrictions still apply to Special Forces

summary.title: Armor Movement
bullets: Move up to 3 hexes and still battle
Fastest unit on the battlefield
Elite Armor starts with 4 figures instead of 3

summary.title: Artillery Movement
bullets: Move 1 hex OR battle -- never both in the same turn
Position carefully; repositioning wastes a turn of firing
Artillery in a bunker may not move from that hex

summary.title: Step 4: Battle
bullets: Resolve battles one unit at a time, in any order
Each unit may battle once per turn (optional)
Cannot split dice between multiple targets
Casualties do not reduce a unit's firepower

summary.title: Close Assault vs. Fire
bullets: Adjacent attack = Close Assault; 2+ hexes away = Fire
A unit adjacent to an enemy must Close Assault (cannot fire past them)
Close Assault triggers Taking Ground and Armor Overrun rules

summary.title: Infantry Battle Range
bullets: Range: up to 3 hexes
Dice: 3 at 1 hex, 2 at 2 hexes, 1 at 3 hexes
Count-down method: finger from adjacent hex toward target

summary.title: Armor Battle Range
bullets: Range: up to 3 hexes
Always rolls 3 dice regardless of distance
Consistent firepower at any range

summary.title: Artillery Battle Range
bullets: Range: up to 6 hexes (longest in the game)
Dice: 3, 3, 2, 2, 1, 1 by distance
Does NOT require line of sight

summary.title: Line of Sight
bullets: Infantry and Armor must have line of sight to target
Blocked by: units, forests, towns, hedgerows, hills, bunkers
Target hex terrain does NOT block line of sight
Artillery ignores line of sight entirely

summary.title: Terrain Battle Dice Reductions
bullets: Forest/Hedgerow/Town/Bunker: Infantry -1 die, Armor -2 dice
Hill (attacking uphill): Infantry -1, Armor -1
Artillery is never reduced by terrain
Check scenario summary cards for specifics

summary.title: Resolving Battle Dice
bullets: Die faces: Infantry (x2), Armor, Grenade, Star, Flag
Matching unit symbol = 1 hit; Grenade = 1 hit on any unit type
Star = miss (unless a Tactic card says otherwise)
Flag = target must retreat 1 hex toward its board edge

summary.title: Retreat
bullets: 1 flag = 1 hex retreat toward own edge; 2 flags = 2 hexes, etc.
Cannot retreat through occupied hexes, impassable terrain, or off the board
If unable to retreat, lose 1 figure per incomplete retreat hex
Bunkers, sandbags, hedgehogs: may ignore the first flag

summary.title: Taking Ground
bullets: Infantry in Close Assault may advance into a vacated hex after eliminating or forcing a retreat
Terrain movement/battle restrictions still apply
Artillery may never take ground

summary.title: Armor Overrun
bullets: Armor in Close Assault may advance into vacated hex AND battle again
If adjacent to new enemy, second battle must be Close Assault
May Take Ground again after Overrun, but only 1 Overrun per turn
Complete all battles (including Overrun) before next unit battles

summary.title: Step 5: Draw a Command Card
bullets: Discard played card, draw 1 new Command card from the deck
If deck is empty, shuffle discards into a new deck
Opponent's turn now begins
Game ends when one player reaches the required Victory Medal count

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Battlefield
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening of the Pegasus Bridge scenario with two players: the Allied player and the Axis player. Pegasus Bridge is the recommended first scenario. The Allied player has 6 Command cards and goes first. The Axis player has only 2 Command cards. The victory condition is 4 medals. Follow along with the moves and watch for common mistakes.

walkthrough.title: The Scenario Briefing
walkthrough.text: Pegasus Bridge takes place on the countryside board. The Allies are launching a surprise nighttime assault to capture two bridges over the river. The terrain includes 20 river hexes, 4 village hexes, and 9 forest hexes. Two bridges span the rivers, protected by barbed wire and sandbags. The Allies need to seize and hold those bridges. The Axis forces are caught off guard with only 2 Command cards, representing their confusion in the opening moments of D-Day.

walkthrough.title: Allied Turn 1: Play a Section Card
walkthrough.text: The Allied player looks at their 6 Command cards and picks a Section card that orders units on the right flank. They play it face up and read it aloud: 'Order 2 units on the right flank.' The Allied player chooses two Infantry units near the eastern bridge. This is a solid opening -- concentrating force on one bridge to secure it quickly.

walkthrough.title: Allied Turn 1: Move and Battle
walkthrough.text: The first ordered Infantry unit moves 1 hex toward the bridge. The second Infantry unit also advances 1 hex, positioning itself adjacent to an Axis Infantry unit defending the bridge approach. Since both units moved only 1 hex, both are eligible to battle. The first unit is not adjacent to any enemy, so it holds fire. The second unit is adjacent to the Axis defenders -- it must Close Assault. The Allied player rolls 3 Battle dice and gets 1 Infantry hit and 1 Flag. The Axis defender loses 1 figure and must retreat 1 hex back. The Allied Infantry takes ground, advancing into the vacated hex. Finally, the Allied player draws a new Command card, bringing their hand back to 6.

walkthrough.title: Axis Turn 1: The Card Shortage
walkthrough.text: Now the Axis player looks at their hand -- just 2 Command cards. This is a painful position. With only 2 cards, the Axis player has very limited options. They play a Section card that orders 1 unit in the center. They move an Infantry unit 1 hex into a forest for cover. Since the unit entered a forest hex, it must stop and cannot battle this turn. The Axis player draws a new card, bringing their hand to 2 again. The Axis player is feeling the pressure of having so few cards.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 1: Forgetting Terrain Restrictions
walkthrough.text: Let's say the Axis player made a mistake here. They moved their Infantry unit into the forest and then tried to also attack with it. Stop! When a unit enters a forest hex, it must stop and may not battle on that turn. This is one of the most common beginner errors. The same restriction applies to hedgerows, towns, and villages: entering these terrain types ends your movement AND prevents you from battling that turn. Always check the terrain summary card before moving into a new hex.

walkthrough.title: Allied Turn 2: Pressing the Attack
walkthrough.text: The Allied player plays another Section card, this time ordering 3 units on the left flank. They advance Infantry toward the western bridge. One Infantry unit moves 1 hex and engages an Axis unit in Close Assault. The Allied player rolls 3 dice: 2 Grenades and a Star. Two hits! The Axis unit is reduced to 2 figures. The Star is a miss. The Allies are making progress on both flanks.

walkthrough.title: Axis Turn 2: Fighting Back
walkthrough.text: The Axis player plays their other Command card -- a Tactic card that allows a counter-attack. An Axis Infantry unit in the village fires at the approaching Allies at 2 hexes range. Being in a village, the Axis unit has good defensive cover if attacked back. The Axis player rolls 2 dice (Infantry at 2 hexes range): 1 Infantry hit and 1 Flag. The Allied unit takes 1 casualty and must retreat 1 hex. The Axis draws a card, back to 2.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 2: Ignoring Line of Sight
walkthrough.text: Here is another common mistake. The Axis player wants to attack an Allied unit on the other side of the forest, but there is a friendly Axis unit standing between them and the target. The player reaches for the dice, but wait -- line of sight is blocked. Any unit, whether friendly or enemy, that stands in the path between attacker and target blocks the line of sight for Infantry and Armor. Always trace that imaginary line from hex center to hex center before rolling dice. If anything stands in the way -- another unit, a forest, a town, a hill -- the shot is blocked. Remember, Artillery is the exception. Artillery never needs line of sight.

walkthrough.title: Allied Turn 3: Seizing a Bridge
walkthrough.text: The Allied player orders units on the right flank again. An Allied Infantry unit, now adjacent to the eastern bridge, Close Assaults the remaining Axis defender. The Allied player rolls 3 dice: 1 Infantry, 1 Grenade, 1 Flag. That is 2 hits plus a retreat. The Axis unit, now reduced to its last figure, takes 2 hits and is eliminated. The Allied player places the final Axis figure on their Medal Stand -- that is 1 Victory Medal. The Allied Infantry takes ground, advancing onto the bridge hex. They now hold a key objective.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 3: Forgetting that Casualties Do Not Reduce Firepower
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake that goes the other way. The Axis player has a damaged Infantry unit with only 2 figures remaining. They assume it rolls fewer dice and decides not to attack with it, thinking it is too weak. Wrong! In Memoir '44, the number of casualties a unit has suffered does not affect its firepower. That 2-figure Infantry unit still rolls 3 dice in Close Assault, 2 dice at 2 hexes, and 1 die at 3 hexes -- exactly the same as a fresh 4-figure unit. Never write off a damaged unit. It hits just as hard as a full-strength one.

walkthrough.title: The Battle Continues
walkthrough.text: Play continues back and forth. The Allies are pushing aggressively to capture both bridges and eliminate Axis units. The Axis player, with fewer Command cards, must be resourceful -- using terrain for cover, retreating when necessary, and picking fights carefully. Each turn follows the same 5-step sequence: play a card, order units, move, battle, draw a card. The rhythm becomes natural after just a few turns.

walkthrough.title: Reaching 4 Medals
walkthrough.text: After several more turns, the Allied player eliminates a third and then a fourth Axis unit. That is 4 Victory Medals -- the Allies win this scenario! The Allied player achieved this by concentrating force on one objective at a time, using terrain for cover during advances, and pressing the advantage of having 6 Command cards versus the Axis player's 2.

walkthrough.title: Swap Sides for the Rematch
walkthrough.text: Now swap sides and play the scenario again. The player who was Allies is now Axis, and vice versa. The winner of the match is whoever earns the most total Victory Medals across both games. This match play format ensures fairness -- even if one side has a historical advantage, both players get to command both armies.

walkthrough.title: What You Learned
walkthrough.text: In this practice game, you experienced the core loop of Memoir '44: play a card, order units, move, battle, draw. You saw how terrain restricts movement and provides cover, how line of sight matters for targeting, and how damaged units are still dangerous. You also learned that match play balances scenario asymmetry.

walkthrough.title: Before You Deploy
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into more scenarios, here are three things that can help.

First -- check out General Tips for strategy basics on card management, terrain use, and combined arms.

Second -- reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every unit type, terrain type, and game term.

And most importantly -- ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Good luck, Commander!

summary.title: Welcome to the Battlefield
bullets: Scripted practice tutorial with 2 players (recommended count for Memoir '44)
Uses the Pegasus Bridge scenario: Allies (6 cards) vs. Axis (2 cards)
Victory condition: first to 4 medals

summary.title: The Scenario Briefing
bullets: Countryside board, nighttime surprise assault on two bridges
Terrain: 20 river, 4 village, 9 forest hexes; 2 bridges, wire, sandbags
Axis starts at a card disadvantage (2 vs. 6)

summary.title: Allied Turn 1: Play a Section Card
bullets: Play a Section card ordering 2 units on the right flank
Choose 2 Infantry units near the eastern bridge
Concentrating force on one objective first is a strong opening

summary.title: Allied Turn 1: Move and Battle
bullets: Move Infantry 1 hex each toward the bridge
Close Assault the adjacent Axis defender: 3 dice
Result: 1 hit + 1 retreat, then Take Ground into vacated hex
Draw a card to refill hand to 6

summary.title: Axis Turn 1: The Card Shortage
bullets: Axis has only 2 Command cards -- very limited options
Order 1 unit in center, move Infantry into a forest for cover
Forest entry = must stop AND cannot battle this turn

summary.title: Mistake 1: Forgetting Terrain Restrictions
bullets: Common error: trying to battle after entering a forest, hedgerow, or town
Rule: entering these terrain types stops movement AND prevents battle that turn
Always check the terrain summary card before moving

summary.title: Allied Turn 2: Pressing the Attack
bullets: Order 3 units on the left flank, advance toward western bridge
Close Assault: 2 Grenades = 2 hits on any unit type
Allies pushing on both flanks simultaneously

summary.title: Axis Turn 2: Fighting Back
bullets: Axis plays a Tactic card for a counter-attack
Infantry fires from a village at 2 hexes: 2 dice
1 Infantry hit + 1 Flag: Allied unit takes casualty and retreats

summary.title: Mistake 2: Ignoring Line of Sight
bullets: Common error: attacking through a hex occupied by another unit
Any unit (friend or foe) in the path blocks line of sight for Infantry/Armor
Forests, towns, hills, bunkers, hedgerows also block
Exception: Artillery never needs line of sight

summary.title: Allied Turn 3: Seizing a Bridge
bullets: Close Assault eliminates the last Axis defender
Place eliminated figure on Medal Stand = 1 Victory Medal
Allied Infantry Takes Ground onto the bridge hex

summary.title: Mistake 3: Forgetting that Casualties Do Not Reduce Firepower
bullets: Common error: assuming damaged units roll fewer dice
Rule: a 2-figure Infantry still rolls 3 dice in Close Assault
Never write off a damaged unit -- it hits just as hard as full strength

summary.title: The Battle Continues
bullets: Play continues alternating turns, same 5-step sequence each time
Allies push for both bridges; Axis uses terrain and retreats carefully
The turn rhythm becomes natural within a few turns

summary.title: Reaching 4 Medals
bullets: Allied player eliminates 4 Axis units = 4 Victory Medals = Allied victory
Won by concentrating force, using terrain, and leveraging card advantage

summary.title: Swap Sides for the Rematch
bullets: Swap sides and replay the scenario
Match winner = most total Victory Medals across both games
Ensures fairness despite historical scenario asymmetry

summary.title: What You Learned
bullets: Core loop: play card, order, move, battle, draw
Terrain restricts movement and provides defensive cover
Line of sight matters; damaged units retain full firepower
Match play balances asymmetric scenarios

summary.title: Before You Deploy
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy on cards, terrain, and combined arms
Reference the Appendix for every unit, terrain, and game term
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime -- tap Q&A for instant help

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, let's cover the most frequent beginner errors. First, trying to battle after moving into a forest, hedgerow, or town -- you cannot do that; entering these terrain types ends your turn for that unit. Second, forgetting line of sight and trying to shoot through friendly units or blocking terrain. Third, assuming damaged units are weaker -- they are not; a single-figure unit rolls the same dice as a full-strength one. Fourth, wasting Command cards by playing Section cards for areas where you have no units. And fifth, neglecting to retreat when you should. Sometimes pulling back preserves a unit that would otherwise be eliminated, denying your opponent a medal.

walkthrough.title: Command Card Management
walkthrough.text: Your hand of Command cards is the most important resource in the game. Having more cards means more options, and options win wars. Never waste a card -- if a Section card does not match where your units are, consider using a Tactic card instead or repositioning units to where you have better card coverage. Hold onto high-value Tactic cards until the moment is right. Cards like Behind Enemy Lines or Armor Assault can swing an entire game if played at the perfect moment. Also pay attention to how many cards your opponent has. If they started with fewer cards, they have fewer options and are more predictable.

walkthrough.title: Use Terrain to Your Advantage
walkthrough.text: Terrain is your best friend in Memoir '44. Forests, hedgerows, towns, and bunkers all reduce the attacker's dice when they shoot at you. Infantry attacking into a forest or town rolls 1 fewer die. Armor rolls 2 fewer dice. That is a massive defensive advantage. Whenever possible, position your units in defensive terrain. When attacking, try to draw enemies out of cover or flank around terrain to avoid the dice reductions. Hills give a defensive bonus to the unit on top but no penalty when units are at the same elevation.

walkthrough.title: Combined Arms
walkthrough.text: The three unit types work best together. Infantry is your workhorse -- cheap, numerous, and capable at all ranges, but slow. Armor is fast and deadly at any distance, making it perfect for flanking and exploiting breakthroughs. Artillery provides long-range support without needing line of sight, softening up enemies before your troops advance. A strong attack combines all three: Artillery weakens the target from behind, Armor punches through on a flank, and Infantry follows up to hold the ground. Do not send one unit type alone against a combined-arms defense.

walkthrough.title: Concentration of Force
walkthrough.text: One of the biggest strategic mistakes is spreading your attacks evenly across the entire battlefield. Instead, concentrate your force on one section at a time. If you have 3 units on the left flank and only 1 on the right, play cards that let you attack on the left. Overwhelming one area lets you eliminate units quickly for medals and creates a breakthrough that can roll up the enemy line. Save your weaker flank for defensive Section cards or Tactic cards that react to enemy moves.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Retreat
walkthrough.text: New players often fight to the last figure in every engagement, but retreating can be the smarter play. A damaged unit that retreats to safety denies your opponent a Victory Medal. A 2-figure Infantry unit in a forest is still dangerous and forces the enemy to spend resources finishing it off. Retreat behind terrain when possible, pulling damaged units back to forests or towns where they are harder to kill. Remember: the game ends on medals, not on territory. Sometimes giving up a hex to save a unit is the winning move.

walkthrough.title: Armor Overrun Awareness
walkthrough.text: The Armor Overrun is one of the most powerful moves in the game. When your Armor eliminates or forces a retreat in Close Assault, it advances and gets a second attack. This can potentially chain into destroying two units in a single turn. Always look for Overrun opportunities -- position your tanks adjacent to weakened enemy units. Conversely, be aware of when your opponent's tanks might Overrun your units. Avoid leaving damaged units adjacent to enemy Armor. Pull them back or screen them with other units to deny the Overrun.

walkthrough.title: Match Play Strategy
walkthrough.text: Since the rulebook recommends match play -- playing the same scenario twice with swapped sides -- think about your total medal count across both games. If you are playing the historically favored side first and winning easily, do not get overconfident. Your opponent will have the same advantage next game. Try to maximize your medal margin when you have the advantage and minimize the damage when you do not. If a scenario seems one-sided, focus on denying medals when you are on the weaker side rather than trying to match the stronger side's offense.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't battle after entering a forest, hedgerow, or town
Always check line of sight before attacking (Infantry/Armor)
Damaged units still roll the same number of dice
Don't waste Section cards on areas with no units
Retreating to deny a medal is often better than fighting to the last figure

summary.title: Command Card Management

Track your opponent's card count for predictability
Reposition units to match your best available cards

Always position defending units in terrain when possible
Flank around terrain to avoid dice reductions on offense
Hills: -1 die when attacking uphill; no penalty at same elevation

summary.title: Combined Arms
bullets: Infantry: slow but numerous and versatile at all ranges
Armor: fast (3 hexes), always 3 dice, perfect for flanking
Artillery: 6-hex range, no line of sight needed, softens targets
Best attacks combine all three unit types working together

summary.title: Concentration of Force
bullets: Focus attacks on one section rather than spreading evenly
Overwhelming one area earns quick medals and creates breakthroughs
Use defensive cards to hold your weaker flank
Play Section cards that match where your strength is

summary.title: Know When to Retreat
bullets: Retreating denies your opponent a Victory Medal
A damaged unit in terrain is still dangerous and hard to finish off
The game ends on medals, not territory
Sometimes giving up a hex to save a unit wins the game

summary.title: Armor Overrun Awareness
bullets: Overrun: Armor eliminates/forces retreat in Close Assault, advances, battles again
Can potentially destroy two units in one turn
Look for Overrun setups; avoid leaving damaged units adjacent to enemy tanks
Screen vulnerable units with other units to block Overruns

summary.title: Match Play Strategy
bullets: Match play: same scenario twice, swapped sides, most total medals wins
Maximize medal margin when you have the advantage
On the weaker side, focus on denying medals rather than full offense
Track total medal count across both games, not just each battle

walkthrough.title: Card Counting and Hand Reading
walkthrough.text: Advanced players track not just their own hand but the tempo of their opponent's plays. If your opponent keeps playing Section cards on one flank, they may be running low on cards for the other sections. When the Command deck has been partially depleted, you can estimate which Tactic cards might still be available. If you have seen three Probe cards already, a fourth is unlikely. Use this information to predict your opponent's options and plan accordingly. The 60-card deck contains 40 Section cards and 20 Tactic cards, so roughly two-thirds of drawn cards will be Section cards.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Initiative
walkthrough.text: Tempo in Memoir '44 means maintaining the initiative -- forcing your opponent to react to your moves rather than executing their own plan. You seize tempo by threatening multiple objectives simultaneously. If you press on the left flank with Infantry while your Armor repositions in the center, your opponent must choose which threat to address. They cannot cover both with a single Section card. When you have more Command cards than your opponent, you can sustain tempo longer because you have more options each turn. Use high-card-count openings (like a 6 vs. 2 start in Pegasus Bridge) to establish tempo early and never let go.

walkthrough.title: Sacrificial Screening
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best use of a unit is not to fight but to screen. A single Infantry figure placed between your valuable units and the enemy's Armor can block an Overrun, absorb a turn of attacks, and deny line of sight. Even a 1-figure unit standing in the path forces the enemy to spend an order and a turn eliminating it before they can reach your key positions. This is especially powerful when defending against Armor -- a sacrificial screen in the center can buy your flanks time to counterattack.

walkthrough.title: Artillery Positioning and Deployment
walkthrough.text: Artillery is the most position-dependent unit in the game. It can fire 6 hexes without line of sight, but moving it costs an entire turn of shooting. The ideal position is behind a hill or in a forest near the center of the board, where it can reach targets across all three sections without ever needing to relocate. Never place Artillery on the front line where it can be engaged in Close Assault -- it only has 2 figures and will die quickly. If a scenario places your Artillery poorly, consider spending one early turn repositioning it. One lost turn of fire is worth several turns of better positioning.

walkthrough.title: Exploiting the Flank Structure
walkthrough.text: The three-section battlefield creates natural choke points and advantages. Units on the section dividing line can be ordered from either section, making them extremely flexible. Advanced players deliberately position key units on the dotted line so they can be activated by either flank or center cards. This doubles the number of Command cards that can reach those units. Conversely, try to trap your opponent's units deep in a single section where they can only be ordered by cards for that specific section. A unit stuck on the far left flank is useless when the Axis player draws only center and right flank Section cards.

walkthrough.title: Retreat Trapping
walkthrough.text: An advanced offensive tactic is to set up retreat traps. If you position your units so that the enemy's retreat path is blocked -- by the board edge, by impassable terrain like rivers, or by your own units cutting off escape routes -- every Flag you roll becomes devastating. Each retreat hex that cannot be completed costs the defender 1 figure. Rolling 2 Flags against a unit trapped against the board edge means 2 extra casualties on top of any hits. On beach scenarios, this is especially brutal because ocean hexes are impassable for retreats.

walkthrough.title: Special Forces Tactics
walkthrough.text: Special Forces Infantry (US Rangers, British Commandos, and similar) can move 2 hexes and still battle, unlike regular Infantry which must choose between moving 2 or battling. This makes Special Forces ideal for aggressive pushes through difficult terrain. They can enter a forest hex and battle on the same turn that regular Infantry would have to stop and wait. Use Special Forces as your spearhead, breaking through terrain that would stall regular units. However, they are still subject to terrain restrictions -- entering a forest still stops them, they just get to fight when they arrive. French Resistance forces are unique: they always battle when entering woods, villages, or hedgerows, but if they move 2 hexes, they cannot battle. They also retreat up to 3 hexes per flag and start with only 3 figures.

walkthrough.title: Elite Armor Tactics
walkthrough.text: Elite Armor units, like German Tiger tank divisions, start with 4 figures instead of the standard 3. This extra figure means they can absorb one additional hit before being eliminated, making them incredibly resilient. Use Elite Armor for aggressive pushes where normal tanks would be too fragile. Their 4-figure durability combined with standard Armor mobility (3 hexes) and firepower (always 3 dice) makes them the most dangerous unit on the board. When facing Elite Armor, prioritize terrain defense and do not engage them in the open. You need 4 hits to destroy them, which means concentrating multiple units is essential.

walkthrough.title: Scenario-Specific Preparation
walkthrough.text: Before each new scenario, study the battle map carefully. Identify the key terrain features that will determine the flow of battle. Where are the choke points? Which terrain provides the best defensive positions? Where are the Victory Medal objectives? Count the number of units on each side and their types. A scenario with 6 Infantry units and no Armor plays very differently from one with balanced forces. Also note the Command card distribution -- if one side starts with 4 cards and the other with 6, the 4-card side needs to play more conservatively. Finally, read the historical notes. They often hint at the optimal strategy for each side.

walkthrough.title: Team Play and Overlord Strategy
walkthrough.text: Memoir '44 supports team play with up to 6 players (3 per side) and an Overlord variant for up to 8 players across multiple boards. In 2-player teams, one player commands the center (Commander-in-Chief) and the other directs both flanks. In 3-player teams, each player controls one section. The Commander-in-Chief plays Tactic cards that affect all sections. Team play adds a communication dimension -- you must coordinate with teammates using the shared cardholder but cannot discuss specific card contents with the enemy listening. Effective team play requires trusting your teammates to handle their sections while the Commander-in-Chief makes macro-level strategic decisions about when and where to commit Tactic cards.

Estimate remaining Tactic cards as the deck depletes

Never on the front line -- only 2 figures, dies fast in Close Assault
6-hex range + no line of sight needed = fire from safety
One early repositioning turn is worth many turns of better angles

Each impossible retreat hex = 1 extra casualty per Flag

bullets: Team play: up to 6 players (3 per side); Overlord: up to 8 on multiple boards

entries.definition: The most common unit type. Composed of 4 figures at full strength. May move 1 hex and battle, or move 2 hexes and not battle. Battle range of 3 hexes with dice count-down: 3, 2, 1. Requires line of sight to attack.
Tank unit composed of 3 figures at full strength. May move up to 3 hexes and still battle. Always rolls 3 Battle dice regardless of distance, up to 3 hexes. Requires line of sight to attack.
Long-range support unit composed of 2 figures at full strength. May move 1 hex OR battle, but not both. Range of 6 hexes with dice count-down: 3, 3, 2, 2, 1, 1. Does NOT require line of sight.

Elite Infantry (US Rangers, British Commandos, etc.) that may move up to 2 hexes and still battle, unlike regular Infantry. Still subject to terrain restrictions. Identified by Special Forces badges on the board.

Unique Special Forces. Start with 3 figures instead of 4. May always battle when entering woods, villages, or hedgerows (where regular Infantry must stop). May retreat up to 3 hexes per flag instead of 1. Unlike other Special Forces, moving 2 hexes means they cannot battle that turn.

Enhanced Armor units such as German Tiger tank divisions. Start with 4 figures instead of the standard 3, representing exceptional durability. Otherwise behave identically to standard Armor: move 3 hexes, always 3 dice.
Allied Special Forces Infantry identified by their shoulder patch badge. May move 2 hexes and still battle. Featured prominently in the Pointe du Hoc scenario.

Allied Special Forces Infantry identified by the Pegasus shoulder patch of the British 6th Airborne Division. May move 2 hexes and still battle. Featured in the Pegasus Bridge and Sword Beach scenarios.
Terrain type. Movement: unit must stop upon entering. Battle: cannot battle the turn it enters. Defense: Infantry -1 die, Armor -2 dice, Artillery not reduced. Blocks line of sight.

Terrain type typical of Normandy. Movement: must be adjacent to enter; must stop upon entering; leaving allows only 1 hex of movement. Battle: cannot battle the turn it enters. Defense: Infantry -1 die, Armor -2 dice, Artillery not reduced. Blocks line of sight.

Terrain type. Movement: no restrictions. Battle: attacking uphill reduces Infantry and Armor by 1 die; Artillery not reduced. Same-height combat has no penalty. Blocks line of sight for units trying to see over it; no block at same height (plateau effect).

Terrain type. Movement: unit must stop upon entering. Battle: cannot battle the turn it enters. Defense: Infantry -1 die, Armor -2 dice, Artillery not reduced. Armor on a town hex also reduces its own attack dice by 2. Blocks line of sight.
Impassable terrain. A unit may only enter a river hex when the hex has a bridge. A unit on a bridge battles as normal. Rivers do not block line of sight.
Fixed obstacle placed on a river hex to allow crossing. Units on a bridge hex battle normally. Bridges are often key Victory Medal objectives in scenarios like Pegasus Bridge.

Terrain type on the beach-side board. Movement: maximum 1 hex; units are assumed on landing craft or in shallow water. Battle: cannot battle on an ocean hex. Impassable for retreat moves. Does not block line of sight.
Terrain type on the beach-side board. Movement: may only move 2 hexes maximum. Battle: no combat restrictions; may still Take Ground after Close Assault. Does not block line of sight.

Fixed obstacle. Only Infantry may move onto a bunker hex; Armor and Artillery cannot enter. Artillery starting on a bunker may not move from it. Defense: Infantry -1 die, Armor -2 dice, Artillery not reduced. Unit may ignore the first flag. Remove 1 Artillery figure per retreat flag. Only the side noted in briefing may claim bunker defense. Blocks line of sight.

Fixed obstacle made of steel rails or wood with anti-tank mines. Only Infantry may enter a hedgehog hex. No movement restrictions for Infantry. No combat restrictions. Unit on a hedgehog may ignore the first flag. Does not block line of sight.

Removable obstacle. No movement restrictions, but sandbags are removed when a unit leaves the hex. Provides 360-degree protection: Infantry and Armor -1 die when attacking a sandbagged unit (if no better terrain protection exists). Unit may ignore the first flag. Artillery not reduced. Does not block line of sight.

Removable obstacle. Movement: unit must stop upon entering a wire hex. Battle: Infantry on wire rolls 1 fewer die. Infantry may remove wire instead of battling. Armor must remove wire but may still battle. Does not block line of sight.

One of two Command card types (40 of 60 total). Specifies which section(s) of the battlefield you may issue orders in and how many units you may order. Sections are left flank, center, and right flank.

One of two Command card types (20 of 60 total). Allows special moves, battles, or actions as described on the card. Some affect one section; others order units across all sections. Hold for decisive moments.

The 60-card deck consisting of 40 Section cards and 20 Tactic cards. You play 1 card per turn to order your units. Cards are kept secret in your cardholder. After resolving your turn, draw 1 new card from the deck.
Custom 6-sided dice with faces: Infantry (appears twice), Armor, Grenade, Star, and Flag. The game includes 8 dice, 4 per side. Rolled during combat to determine hits and retreats.
Appears on 2 of 6 die faces. Scores 1 hit when the target is an Infantry unit. The most common hit symbol on the dice.
Appears on 1 of 6 die faces. Scores 1 hit when the target is an Armor unit.
Appears on 1 of 6 die faces. Scores 1 hit on any unit type: Infantry, Armor, or Artillery. The universal hit symbol.
Appears on 1 of 6 die faces. Normally a miss. Used by a limited number of Tactic Command cards for special effects.

Appears on 1 of 6 die faces. Forces the target unit to retreat 1 hex toward its own board edge. Multiple flags force multiple retreat hexes. If retreat is impossible, the unit loses 1 figure per incomplete hex.

Awarded when an enemy unit is completely eliminated. Place the last enemy figure on one of your Medal Stands. Some scenarios also award medals for holding terrain objectives. The game includes 10 double-sided Victory Medals.
Spaces on the bottom left edge of your side of the board where you place eliminated enemy unit figures. Each figure placed represents 1 Victory Medal earned.

An attack against an adjacent enemy hex. A unit adjacent to an enemy must Close Assault that enemy and cannot fire at a more distant target. Triggers Taking Ground (Infantry) and Armor Overrun (Armor) rules.
An attack against an enemy unit more than 1 hex away. Contrasted with Close Assault. Subject to line of sight for Infantry and Armor. Does not trigger Taking Ground or Armor Overrun.

When an ordered Infantry or Armor unit eliminates or forces retreat of an enemy in Close Assault, it may advance into the vacated hex. Terrain restrictions still apply. Artillery may never take ground.

When an ordered Armor unit takes ground after a successful Close Assault, it may battle again immediately. If adjacent to a new enemy, the second battle must be Close Assault. Maximum 1 Overrun per Armor unit per turn. May Take Ground again after Overrun.

An imaginary line from the center of the attacker's hex to the center of the target hex. Blocked by units, forests, towns, hedgerows, hills, and bunkers in the path. Target hex terrain does not block. Artillery ignores line of sight entirely.

The board is divided by two red dotted lines into three sections: left flank, center, and right flank. Section cards specify which section(s) you may order units in. Each player sees flanks from their own perspective.
The leftmost section of the battlefield from your perspective. Defined by the red dotted line nearest your left. Your left flank is your opponent's right flank.
The middle section of the battlefield between the two red dotted lines. Hexes where a dotted line passes through count as both the flank and center section.
The rightmost section of the battlefield from your perspective. Your right flank is your opponent's left flank.

The two red dotted lines on the board that separate the three sections. A hex through which a dotted line passes is considered part of both adjacent sections, allowing the unit to be ordered from either one.

Forced movement toward your own board edge caused by Flag dice results. 1 flag = 1 hex back. Cannot retreat through occupied hexes, impassable terrain, or off the board. Terrain does not affect retreat movement. If unable to retreat, lose 1 figure per incomplete hex.

Defensive benefit of terrain. When attacking a unit in protective terrain, the attacker rolls fewer dice. The specific reduction depends on terrain type and attacking unit type. Reductions are not cumulative when an obstacle sits on terrain -- only the best protection applies.
Terrain that cannot be moved through. Rivers (without bridges) and oceans (for retreat moves) are impassable. Units cannot retreat through impassable terrain; each blocked hex costs 1 figure.

Physical tokens placed on the board to distinguish Special Forces or Elite units from standard units in the same scenario. The game includes 14 badges total. Rounded corners on a unit's map symbol denote Special Forces or Elite status.
Card stand assembled from 3 snap-together sections per army (6 sections total). Used to hold Command cards on the board edges. Particularly useful for team play and teaching demonstrations.
Double-sided hexagonal game board, 13 hexes wide by 9 hexes deep. One side depicts countryside terrain, the other beach landings. Divided into 3 sections by red dotted lines.

Double-sided hexagonal pieces placed on the board to create the battle scenario's landscape. The game includes 44 terrain hex pieces covering forests, hedgerows, hills, towns, rivers, oceans, and beaches.

Reference cards displayed beside the board. 7 terrain cards summarize terrain effects. 1 double-sided unit summary card covers standard and special units. 1 double-sided obstacle summary card covers bunkers, hedgehogs, sandbags, and wire. 9 total.

Recommended format where players play the same scenario twice, swapping sides for the second game. The winner is the player with the most total Victory Medals across both battles. Balances historical advantages.

Multiplayer variant for up to 6 players (3 per side). In 2-player teams: one commands the center (Commander-in-Chief), the other directs flanks. In 3-player teams: each player controls one section. Commander-in-Chief plays Tactic cards for all sections.

Large-scale variant combining multiple Memoir '44 sets. Two or three boards form one oversized battlefield for up to 8 players (4 per side). Requires the supplemental Overlord rules booklet available at memoir44.com.

Simplified version for young children. Remove all Tactic cards from the deck (keep only Section cards). Optionally remove Taking Ground and Armor Overrun rules. Shuffle Section cards when the deck is exhausted.
One side of the double-sided battlefield board, depicting open terrain for inland battles. Used by scenarios like Pegasus Bridge, Mont Mouchet, and Liberation of Paris.
One side of the double-sided battlefield board, depicting a coastal assault with ocean and beach hexes. Used by scenarios like Sword Beach and Omaha Beach.
